title: Dhaba-Style Shahi Paneer
tagline: Restaurant-grade paneer in a rich onion-tomato-cashew gravy, finished with a home-roasted dry masala.
servingsNote: Generous 2 servings; scale the gravy up freely for a crowd
macroNotes: Heads up — at ~1050 kcal a serving this eats well over half a 1600–1800 kcal day, and almost all of it is fat from paneer + ghee + cream. Delicious, but treat it as a treat meal.

Concrete cuts: use 2 tbsp ghee total instead of 6, skip the finishing cream, and pan-sear (or air-fry) the paneer dry instead of frying it in 2–3 tbsp mustard oil → saves ~350–400 kcal per serving without hurting flavour.

Protein is already strong (~40 g/serving from 400 g paneer). If you want more without more fat, stir in 40 g of soaked, squeezed, chopped soya chunks with the sautéed paneer at step 8 — adds ~10 g protein per serving and soaks up the gravy well.

items: 1 heaped tbsp degi/Kashmiri red chilli powder

ingredients.group: For the dry masala (makes a batch — you only need ~1 tbsp here, store the rest)

items: 400 g paneer — cut ~350 g into cubes, keep ~50 g aside to grate
1 tbsp tender coriander stems, finely chopped

steps.title: Temper for the gravy
steps.body: Heat 2 tbsp ghee in a handi/heavy pot. Once hot, add 2 cloves, 1 black cardamom, 2 green cardamoms and 6–7 black peppercorns and let them splutter.

steps.title: Soften the onions
steps.body: Add 1 inch chopped ginger, 1 diagonally-cut green chilli and 2 large chopped onions. Cook until the onions turn translucent.

steps.title: Bloom the spices
steps.body: Add 1 heaped tbsp degi red chilli powder, 2 tbsp coriander powder and 1/4 tsp turmeric. Sauté for 1 minute so the spices bloom in the ghee.

steps.title: Cook the tomatoes
steps.body: Add 2 diced tomatoes, salt to taste and 1/2 cup beaten curd. Cook until the tomatoes go completely mushy and the fat starts to separate.

steps.title: Simmer & blend
steps.body: Add 8 cashews and 1.5 cups water, cover, and simmer 10–12 minutes on medium. Cool to room temperature (important — hot liquid in a blender splatters), then blend to a smooth gravy. Set aside.

steps.title: Roast the dry masala
steps.body: In a dry pan on low heat, roast 4–5 black cardamoms, 7–8 green cardamoms, 2 tbsp coriander seeds, 1.5 tbsp cumin, 1 heaped tbsp black peppercorns, 3–4 cloves, 2 tsp fennel and 1/4 tsp salt for 3–4 minutes until aromatic. Turn off the heat, add 2–3 tbsp kasuri methi, toss, then grind to a fine powder. This makes more than you need — store the extra in a jar (it is a fragrant, fennel-forward blend, NOT plain garam masala).

steps.title: Start the paneer sauté
steps.body: In a pan, heat 2 tbsp mustard oil until it just smokes, then cool it slightly and add 1/2 tsp cumin; let it splutter. Add 1 slit green chilli, 1 chopped onion, 1 chopped capsicum, 1 chopped tomato and 1 tbsp coriander stems. Cook 7–8 minutes on high until softened and lightly charred.

steps.title: Coat the paneer
steps.body: Add 1 tsp degi red chilli powder, 1 tbsp prepared dry masala and the ~350 g paneer cubes. Sauté on medium until every cube is coated and lightly golden. Set aside.

steps.title: Build the final curry
steps.body: In the same handi, add 1 cup water and the blended gravy and bring to a simmer. Stir in the ~50 g grated paneer (it melts in and thickens the gravy), salt to taste and 1 tsp sugar. Cook 5–6 minutes.

steps.title: Combine & finish
steps.body: Fold the sautéed paneer into the gravy. Finish with 1 tbsp ghee and 1–2 tbsp chopped coriander. Transfer to a dish and garnish with 1/4 tsp crushed kasuri methi and 1–2 tbsp fresh cream.

steps.title: Serve
steps.body: Serve hot with roti, naan or kulcha.

notes: The 'dry masala' is a custom home blend (fennel + kasuri methi heavy) — it is not garam masala, though it plays a similar finishing role. The batch keeps for weeks in an airtight jar.
A hand blender works directly in the pot for the gravy — just cool it slightly first and keep the head fully submerged to avoid splatter.
Amul/salted paneer plus salted gravy can get salty fast; salt the gravy conservatively and adjust at the end.
source: Adapted from my own cooking notes.
